figures.caption: Fig. 11: Depth maps on the bridge scene using self-estimated parameters. Each depth map is scaled to match the ground truth range, with near-far corresponding to blueyellow. COLMAP performs relatively well on the road surface of the bridge and in the foreground, but the suspension cables are estimated to be far too thick. the learning based methods capture the shape of the bridge from this view, but perform poorly in the background.

Fig. 10: Depth maps on the crane scene using self-estimated parameters. Each depth map is scaled to match the ground truth range, with near-far corresponding to blueyellow. COLMAP is unable to estimate the majority of the frame accurately, whereas transformer-based methods demonstrate good performance in the foreground. However, in the background, performance degrades.
Fig. 8: Representative frames from the crane scene.
Fig. 9: Representative frames from the bridge scene.

Fig. 1: We propose a new benchmark for 3D reconstruction from imagery captured in asset inspection operations. Given user-specified surface soiling, drone trajectories and asset meshes, we generate synthetic data using Blender [3] that matches the close proximity and high overlap of data captured in industrial asset inspection operations. After adding emulated signal-dependent sensor noise, we benchmark the camera pose and 3D reconstruction accuracy of both end-to-end transformer architectures and traditional structure from motion and multi-view stereo pipelines with ground truth data from our simulator.
Fig. 2: Renders of the office building, crane and bridge scenes. The office scene was rendered with four surface conditions corresponding to very low, low, medium and high levels of soiling.

Fig. 15: Point clouds of the crane scene. COLMAP produces very sparse outputs of the crane, mainly reconstructing the background, whereas the other methods mainly reconstruct the crane, with varying degrees of accuracy. Depth Anything 3 produces the most accurate point clouds, while π3 is somewhat misaligned. VGGT produces unrecognisable output.

Fig. 14: Point clouds of the office building with very low soiling settings. Depth Anything 3 and COLMAP produce the most accurate point clouds. Note that COLMAP does not place points in the windowed areas, whereas Depth Anything 3 does. π3 is somewhat misaligned, and VGGT is strongly misaligned.
Fig. 4: Qualitative camera pose results on the crane scene. COLMAP, GLOMAP, Depth Anything 3 and π3 performed relatively well at estimating the camera poses. However, VGGT performed poorly.
Fig. 5: Point clouds from each method on the bridge scene.

Table 2: Comparison of evaluated methods. ✓ indicates required input and supported output while ✗ indicates unsupported inputs and outputs, and ∼ refers to optional input. PC refers to dense pointcloud reconstructions and Intrin. and Extrin. are camera intrinsics and extrinsics respectively.
Fig. 12: Camera pose results on the bridge scene. All methods perform poorly.
Fig. 13: Camera pose results on the office scene with the very low soiling setting. COLMAP performed best, followed by (in order) GLOMAP, Depth Anything 3, π3 and VGGT.
Fig. 6: The same frame of the office building scene with the four different soiling settings.
Fig. 7: Representative frames from the office building scene.

Table 1: Comparison between our dataset and existing 3D reconstruction and drone imagery datasets. This table compares whether each dataset is captured in an asset inspection style, whether varying levels of surface condition (reflectivity or smudging in our case) are present with the same geometry and what ground truth data is available. Note, Cam. refers to camera parameters. Scenes with an asterisk have variable lighting or environmental conditions.
